summary: Compiler that turns software hotspots into verified FPGA co-processors.
editor_note: This is software-to-silicon with the abstraction pointed at developers, not RTL teams. BoolSi marks a hot C/C++ function, emits a co-processor, driver and bitstream, then checks it against source behavior; its regex demo goes from 2.66 ms on ARM to 0.042 ms with 8 agents. Unlike HLS tools for chip specialists, the bet is that hardware becomes a build artifact. The risk is private-beta reality versus the benchmark.

product_evidence.claim: The homepage says BoolSi marks a hot C or C++ function and synthesizes it into custom digital logic beside the CPU.
The site says the platform maps API calls, updates the BSP, produces the hardware bitstream, and verifies each generated agent against source behavior.
The seed announcement says BoolSi trains ML models to learn program behavior and converge into analyzable digital circuits for FPGAs.

market_context.primary_user: Embedded software teams in robotics and devices whose C or C++ workloads are bottlenecked on CPUs or microcontrollers.
market_context.differentiation: BoolSi exposes hardware acceleration as a compiler output for software developers instead of an HDL project for chip specialists.
market_context.why_now: Robotics and edge devices need lower latency without every software team becoming a custom silicon team.

risks.claim: The key open question is whether private-beta customers see reliable gains outside controlled benchmark workloads and FPGA-friendly functions.
risks.basis: VentureDex editorial assessment based on official product evidence and funding-source review.
